claim: Houston FOG pretreatment for food-service establishments is governed by Houston Code of Ordinances Chapter 47 (Water and Sewers), Article XI, Division 9; cleaning/maintenance rules are in Sec. 47-512. The 'Chapter 47' assumption is correct but the live section is 47-512.
notes: Article XI title is 'Transportation and Treatment of Certain Wastes', not a 'grease trap' article.

claim: Houston requires full evacuation of each grease interceptor/holding tank at least once every 90 days (Sec. 47-512(b)), with a hard floor of at least once every 180 days that even a waiver cannot eliminate (Sec. 47-512(f)).
notes: 90-day requirement waivable via approved Notice of Waiver; 180-day floor is not.

claim: Houston's pump-out trigger is 25% of the WETTED HEIGHT (measured bottom of device to invert of outlet pipe) containing floating materials, sediment, oils or greases — a depth measure, not 25% of volume (Sec. 47-512(c)).
notes: In Houston the 25% condition triggers cleaning MORE frequently than every 90 days; it is an accelerator above the 90-day baseline, not the baseline itself.

claim: Dallas requires grease interceptors cleaned not less than every 90 days, or whenever 25% or more of the interceptor contains floating materials, sediment, oils, or grease, per Dallas City Code Chapter 19, Sec. 19-126.2(c).
notes: Official Dallas Water Utilities FAQ cites the codified subsection verbatim. Dallas brochure loosely says '25% of design capacity'; the code-citing FAQ says '25% ... contains floating materials, sediment, oils, or grease' (FOG+solids basis, like Houston/SAWS).

claim: San Antonio (SAWS) requires grease interceptor cleaning at a frequency not less often than every 90 days, and within 2 working days whenever 25% or more of the wetted height (bottom of device to invert of outlet pipe) contains floating materials, sediment, fats, oils, or grease — San Antonio City Code Sec. 34-527(b) (SAWS FOG Ordinance).
notes: Same 25% wetted-height / 90-day formula as Houston, plus an explicit 2-working-day response window.

claim: Austin requires grease traps pumped out at least once every 90 days, or sooner if grease and solids make up 50% OR MORE of the wetted height of the trap (Austin City Code Sec. 15-10-197). Austin's trigger is 50%, NOT 25%.
notes: Key per-jurisdiction deviation: Austin shares the 90-day interval but uses a 50% wetted-height trigger.

claim: The '90-day interval' is shared by all four Texas cities, but the percent-full trigger is NOT universal: Houston, Dallas, and San Antonio use 25%; Austin uses 50%. Therefore trigger percent must be configured per-jurisdiction.
notes: Measurement basis is wetted height for Houston/SAWS/Austin; Dallas code-FAQ phrases it as '25% of the interceptor' (FOG+solids).

claim: Hauling grease-trap and grit-trap waste in Texas is governed by Title 30 TAC Chapter 312 (Sludge Use, Disposal, and Transportation); transporter registration is 30 TAC §312.142 and the trip-ticket/manifest + recordkeeping rule is 30 TAC §312.145.
notes: Section numbers verified. TCEQ 2014 guidance RG-086 labels them 'Subchapter G' while Cornell LII files them under 'Subchapter F'; cite by section number, which is not in dispute.

claim: A TCEQ transporter registration is mandatory before hauling grease/grit trap waste; the executive director annotates the registration authorization with an assigned registration number, and a current copy must be kept at the place of business and in EACH vehicle operated under that registration (30 TAC §312.142(a),(c)).
notes: TCEQ RG-389 adds an operational dated-sticker-per-vehicle expectation. This TCEQ-assigned registration number is the value for trucks.registration_ref.

claim: 30 TAC §312.145 requires a trip ticket for each collection/deposit capturing: transporter name/address/phone + commission registration number; generator name/signature/address/phone + date collected; type and amount of waste; signatures of responsible persons collecting/transporting/depositing; date and place deposited; receiving facility identification (permit/site-registration number, location, operator); receiving-facility representative signature acknowledging receipt and amount received; and the volume of the grease/grit trap or septic tank.
notes: Three required signatures: generator, transporter/responsible persons, and receiving-facility on-site representative. Signatures may be electronic for generator and facility (per (a)(2) and (a)(7)).

claim: Under 30 TAC §312.145(b) trip tickets must be divided into five parts (generator at pickup; receiving facility; transporter retains one; one returned by transporter to the generator within 15 days after deposit; one to local authority if needed); copies must be retained for five years; transporters must file an annual summary by July 1 covering June 1–May 31.
notes: Maps to custody_events workflow: copy distribution, returned_to_generator_at (<=15 days), 5-year retention, annual reporting job.

claim: Houston's most recent amendment to Sec. 47-512 is Ord. No. 2020-1024 (Dec. 2, 2020); Houston's FOG discharge limit was reduced from 400 mg/L to an average of 200 mg/L (Sec. 47-514), effective Dec. 2, 2023.
notes: Confirms the quoted 47-512 text is current, not superseded; 2023 Health Dept page noted further proposed changes (proposals only).

claim: The exact $10 per-vehicle authorization-sticker fee cited for TCEQ transporter registration could not be independently verified against current verbatim 30 TAC §312.142 text; it came via a summarizer. The existence of a dated per-vehicle sticker requirement is corroborated by TCEQ RG-389.
notes: Treat the exact fee figure as guidance-level until confirmed against current rule/fee schedule.
